verses.text: 25.1–29.27 Durante o reinado de Ezequias (728–686 a.C.), sábios (conselheiros) reuniram estes provérbios de Salomão e os acrescentaram à coleção.
25.2-3 Esta advertência lembra aos jovens iniciantes no serviço régio que algumas coisas não podem ser compreendidas, inclusive os caminhos por vezes misteriosos do rei (p.ex., 2Sm 11.14-25; 24.3).
25.2-3 Esta advertência lembra aos jovens iniciantes no serviço régio que algumas coisas não podem ser compreendidas, inclusive os caminhos por vezes misteriosos do rei (p.ex., 2Sm 11.14-25; 24.3).
25.4-5 Assim como a prata fica pronta para o uso depois de refinada, a corte do rei é capaz de fazer justiça quando os ímpios são removidos. Um pouco de mal arruína muito bem (Ec 10.1).
25.4-5 Assim como a prata fica pronta para o uso depois de refinada, a corte do rei é capaz de fazer justiça quando os ímpios são removidos. Um pouco de mal arruína muito bem (Ec 10.1).
25.6-7 Para conseguir audiência com o rei, o sábio prefere a humildade ao orgulho (cf. Mt 20.20-28).
25.7 As ações precipitadas costumam terminar em vergonha.

25:8 Go not forth hastily to strive,.... To go to law with a neighbour; think well of it beforehand; consider the nature of the cause, whether right or wrong; or whether it is a matter of such moment as to go to law about; whether it will not be deemed a frivolous and vexatious suit; whether able to bear the expenses of it, and what may probably be the success of it; lest thou know not what to do in the end thereof;

25:9 Debate thy cause with thy neighbour himself,.... Between thee and him alone; lay the matter before him, and hear what he has to say for himself, by which you will better judge of the nature of the cause; try to compromise things, and make up the difference between you, which is much better than to commence a lawsuit; at least such a step should be taken first; see Matt 5:25; and discover not a secret to another; if the thing in controversy is a secret, do not acquaint another person with it;

25:10 Lest he that heareth it put thee to shame,.... Or, "reproach thee" (c) with treachery and deceit. Either the person of whom it is told, or the person to whom it is told; who may make thee ashamed, either by fixing the odious character of a defamer, a whisperer, and backbiter, on thee; or by making a retaliation, and in his turn make known some secret things concerning thyself, which before were not known, and, now published, will be to thy disgrace;
25.11-12 O conselho oportuno e a crítica válida são preciosos, belos e raros (15.23; 26.7,9), e devem ser bem recebidos (10.17; 13.1,10; 15.24,31-32; 17.10).
25.11-12 O conselho oportuno e a crítica válida são preciosos, belos e raros (15.23; 26.7,9), e devem ser bem recebidos (10.17; 13.1,10; 15.24,31-32; 17.10).
25.13 A neve era alívio bem-vindo nos verões quentes e secos de Israel (cf. 26.1); mensageiros confiáveis refrigeram o patrão, permitindo-lhe planejar o futuro.
25.14 Em Israel, a chuva é rara. Quando ela não vem após as nuvens, frusta como quem promete e nada cumpre. Cf. Judas descrevendo falsos mestres (Jd 12).

25:15 By long forbearing is a prince persuaded,.... To come into measures, and do that which his council and ministry advise him to, and to which he may seem at first very averse; but by a mild and gentle representation of things, by an humble submission of them to him, and by frequent remonstrances and patient waiting, his mind is softened, bent, and inclined to take their advice, and pursue the measures suggested to him; which, had they been pressed with heat, haughtiness, and haste, would have been rejected;
25.16-17 Mel com moderação é saboroso e saudável (24.13-14); em excesso, provoca náusea. O princípio aplica-se a outras áreas: é bom visitar o vizinho, mas não em demasia.
25.16-17 Mel com moderação é saboroso e saudável (24.13-14); em excesso, provoca náusea. O princípio aplica-se a outras áreas: é bom visitar o vizinho, mas não em demasia.
25.18 A mentira pode ter consequências letais (6.16-19; 14.5,25).

25:19 Confidence in an unfaithful man in time of trouble,.... It is not good to put confidence in any man, not in princes, nor in the best of men; much less in an unfaithful, prevaricating, and treacherous man; and especially in a time of distress and trouble, depending on his help and assistance, which is leaning on a broken reed, and trusting to a broken staff. Or, "the confidence of an unfaithful man in time of trouble" (o); that which he puts confidence in;

25:20 As he that taketh away a garment in cold weather,.... Either takes it off of himself, or another person, when it would be rather more proper to put another garment on, and so is exposed to the injury of cold weather; and as vinegar upon nitre: nitre was found in Egypt, beyond Memphis, as Strabo says (p); there were two mines of nitre, which produced much, and thence it was called the Nitriotic Nome: others say, nitre has its name from Nitria, a town in Egypt (q), which gives name to the Nitrian desert, where there is a lake called Latron;
25.21-22 Contrariando a expectativa, a compaixão para com o inimigo é mais eficaz que a ira (Rm 12.20).
25.21-22 Contrariando a expectativa, a compaixão para com o inimigo é mais eficaz que a ira (Rm 12.20).

25:23 The north wind driveth away rain,.... So the geographer (w) says, the swift north wind drives away the moist clouds; which usually come from the opposite quarter, the south. The word used has the signification of conceiving, and begetting, and bringing forth; hence some (x) render it to a different sense, and so the Targum, "the north wind bringeth forth rain;'' and in this sense Gersom interprets it, and says, "the north wind produces rain in Jerusalem, because it brings there the vapours that ascend from the sea, which lies north unto it;'' and the philosopher (y) says, that in the northern parts of the world the south wind produces rain;
25.24 Este provérbio repete 21.9.

25:25 A righteous man falling dozen before the wicked,.... Either falling into calamity and distress by means of the wicked man, through his malice and cunning, and which be seeing, rejoices at; or crouching unto him, bowing before him, yielding to him, not daring to oppose or reprove him; or falling into sin in his presence, which he ever after reproaches him for, and openly exposes him, so that his usefulness is lost; and especially if he joins with the wicked man in his course of living;
25.26 A nascente ou fonte de água doce dá vida. Quando os piedosos permitem que os ímpios os dominem, o que era vivificador torna-se mortífero.
25.27-28 O sábio tem domínio próprio e dificilmente é dominado.
25.27-28 O sábio tem domínio próprio e dificilmente é dominado.
